title: Chapter 39: The Graduation
content: The organ music swells and I lose it completely.

She's walking across the stage — cap slightly crooked, gown a size too big because she's still so slight, still looks sixteen even though she's not sixteen anymore, even though she's twenty-two and just became the first person in her family to graduate from university. The tassel swings with each step. Her name echoes through the Exam Hall at Trinity College and the applause is polite and Irish and subdued because this is Dublin and we don't do excessive, but I am doing excessive. I am doing excessive all over my face.
A tissue appears in my peripheral vision.

Julian doesn't even glance at me. His eyes are still on the stage, on the student crossing toward the dean, and his hand just — extends, like a vending machine dispensing exactly the right product. Because he's been married to me for two and a half years and he knows the exact ratio of proud-crying to sad-crying, and this is one hundred percent proud-crying, and he came prepared. There are more tissues in his jacket pocket. I know this because I did the laundry and found a crumpled wad of them in every single pocket, and when I asked, he said, "Contingency planning."

I take the tissue. I dab my eyes. The paper is soft, the cheap institutional kind that feels like it's one step removed from a paper towel, and it clings to the wet on my lashes and I don't care. I don't care because she's shaking the dean's hand and her smile is visible from row fourteen and her parents are three rows ahead of me and her mother is crying too, and I think: this. This is what I chose all of it for. Not just this. But this is part of it.

Three years. Three years since the Choice, since the Garden, since I stood in a space between worlds and decided which life I wanted. Three years since I stopped running from forks and started walking down one. And now I'm sitting in an oak-paneled hall in Dublin watching a kid I taught to code walk across a stage I never walked across myself, and I have never been happier to cry.
Julian's thumb traces the back of my hand. Once. A silent *I see you.*

After the ceremony, the crowd spills onto Front Square like a punctured balloon. Gowns and flowers and parents with cameras and graduates with dazed expressions, the particular daze of people who just realized the thing they worked toward for four years is actually over. It's warm for Dublin — almost twenty degrees, which here counts as a heat wave — and the light hits the cobblestones and fractures into a hundred directions.
I'm scanning the crowd when something hits me at approximately the velocity of a small car.
"I wouldn't be here without you."

Her arms are around my neck and her cap has fallen off and she smells like hairspray and sweat and the particular floral perfume that nineteen-year-olds think is sophisticated and thirty-year-olds remember with fondness. We nearly topple — my heel catches on a cobblestone, Julian's hand shoots out to steady my back — but we don't fall. We just stand there, tangled, while the crowd flows around us like water around a stone.
I shake my head against her shoulder. "You would. You just would have taken a different path to get here."
She pulls back. Her mascara is smudged — she's been crying too — and she laughs, this full-body thing that makes her gown ripple. "You always say that. About everything."
"Because it's true."

And I mean it. Not as comfort. Not as the thing you say to be humble or kind. As fact. There are always other paths. Always. The world doesn't run on single tracks. That's the one thing I learned from glitching through fate — the universe is a branching tree, and every branch reaches the sky eventually. You don't need to walk all of them. You just need to walk one well.

She looks at me the way she always does when I say stuff like this — half exasperated, half like she's filing it away to use later. She's still holding my hands. Her palms are warm and a little sticky from the humidity.
"Come to dinner," she says. "Mam's making lasagne. She makes it for every celebration. It's not even Irish. She just really likes lasagne."

"We'll be there," Julian says, because he is better at saying yes than I am. I'm still standing here thinking about paths and branches and the sixteen-year-old who showed up to my digital skills workshop in Cork with a secondhand laptop and a glare that could curdle milk, who is now a Trinity graduate with an offer from a Dublin tech firm and a family whose trajectory just shifted under her feet.
She was always going to get here. The path just would have been rougher. And there's no shame in a smoother road.

We walk through Trinity's campus and I don't want to leave. Not yet. The stone buildings rise on either side of us, old in the way that European things are old — casually, accidentally, like aging is what happens when you're busy doing other things. The cobblestones are uneven beneath my shoes, and I feel each one through the thin soles, a gentle massage of stone. The River Liffey is somewhere to the north; I can't see it from here but I can smell it, that particular water-and-city scent that Dublin has, briny and dusty at once. Julian's hand is in mine, warm, his thumb still doing that absent tracing thing he does when we walk.

I think about my own graduation. Three years ago. In a different world — literally. The Avery who stood at that ceremony, clutching her diploma like it was a grenade, terrified of what came next. Terrified of choosing. Terrified that every decision would close off every other decision and she'd be stuck with one life, one self, one outcome, and what if it was wrong?

That Avery couldn't have imagined this. Standing in Dublin with a husband who was once a fugitive. A student who just changed her family's trajectory. A life that she chose — not the one she defaulted into, not the one she stumbled toward while looking the other way, but the one she selected with full knowledge of what she was giving up.
She chose without fear.
Not without sadness. Fear and sadness are different. She was sad about the paths she'd never walk. But she wasn't afraid anymore. And that — that was the whole point.
"You're quiet," Julian says.
"I'm always quiet."

"You're quiet in a specific way." He knows my silences the way a sommelier knows wine. There's the contemplative silence, the annoyed silence, the I'm-about-to-say-something-weird silence. "You're thinking about something heavy."
"Maybe I'm just enjoying the walk."
"Avery."
"Fine. I'm thinking about forks."
He doesn't miss a beat. "The app or the utensil?"
"The utensil."
"I'm going to need more context."

I don't give it to him yet. I let the walk continue, let the campus move through us — a group of students lounging on the grass, the bell tower catching the sun, a dog running across the quad with absolutely no regard for the dignity of the institution. The air tastes like summer in Ireland, which is to say it tastes like rain that's briefly pretending to be sunshine. Cool and bright and fleeting.
Julian stops at a bench.
"Here," he says. "Look familiar?"
I look.

It's an ordinary bench. Stone seat, iron frame, the kind of bench that exists in every park and every campus and every place where people need to sit and rest and think. Green paint flaking from the iron in places, a thin seam of lichen along the stone's edge where the rain pools.
But.
Yes.

It looks like the bench in the Garden. The one where the Composite sat, his form shifting through versions of everyone I'd ever known, his voice layered like a choir. The bench where he told me to choose. Not which path to take, but where I was most myself. As if choosing who to be was more important than choosing what to do.

This bench isn't that bench. That bench was in a space between worlds, a Garden that existed outside of time and possibility, and this is just a bench in Dublin. But it looks the same. The proportions. The angle of the iron arms. The way the stone catches the light.
I sit down.

The stone is warm from the afternoon sun, and I feel it through my dress, and I close my eyes, and I'm not back in the Garden — I'm here, I'm present, the cobblestones are real and the students are real and the birds are real — but I feel the same thing I felt in that space between worlds. The weight of all the paths I didn't take.
Not as grief.
As gratitude.

Every path that wasn't this one taught me something. Every Julian who wasn't this Julian — the one who ran, the one who stayed, the one who died, the one who forgot — every version of him revealed something about the man sitting next to me now. The man who packs tissues in every pocket because he knows I'll cry at graduations. The man who falls asleep on trains. The man who engages with my weirdness instead of merely tolerating it.
I open my eyes.
Julian is watching me with that expression he gets — the one that's half concerned and half curious, like I'm a puzzle he's still solving after all these years and he likes the challenge.
"What are you thinking?" he asks.
"I'm thinking about forks."
He raises an eyebrow. "You mentioned."
"Not the app. Actual forks. You know what a fork is, fundamentally?"
"A utensil with prongs used for—"

"Not a choice between two things. That's what everyone thinks. A fork in the road. Pick one direction, mourn the other. But that's not what a fork is for." I look at him. "It's a tool for eating. For taking what you need and leaving the rest."
Julian stares at me.

The breeze picks up. It carries the smell of cut grass and the faintest edge of the sea. A group of students passes behind us, laughing about something, their voices bright and unburdened. The iron arm of the bench is cool under my palm. Julian's eyes haven't left my face.
"That's either profound or unhinged," he says.
"It's both." I lean my head against his shoulder. "That's my brand."

He laughs. Really laughs — not the controlled exhale he does in public, but the full thing, the one that shakes his shoulders and makes him tip his head back toward that impossible sky. And I think, with a clarity that feels like bells: I made the right choice. Not between timelines. Not between one version of the world and another. Between being someone who's afraid of forks and someone who uses them to eat.

We walk back to the train station as the afternoon tilts toward evening. Dublin does something to the light at this hour — it turns amber, then copper, then that particular shade of Irish blue that only exists after rain, when the clouds pull back and the sky is so clean it hurts to look at. The kind of blue that doesn't just reflect light but generates it, like the sky remembered what color it was supposed to be and got enthusiastic about it.

Pearse Station. We board the Cork train and find our usual seats — left side, facing forward, because I get trainsick if I face backward and Julian pretends he doesn't mind but he definitely also prefers it. The carriage smells like fabric softener and coffee and that specific metallic tang of Irish Rail. The seats are that navy-blue velvet that's seen better decades.
Julian is asleep before we clear the city limits.

He always does this. Falls asleep on trains within minutes, like someone flipped a switch. "The rhythm of the train," he says, like it's a feature and not a bug. Like his body was designed specifically for the rocking motion of rail travel. His head tips toward the window, then slides gradually toward my shoulder, and I let it. His breath is warm and slow against my collarbone, and I can feel the faint rumble of it, the machinery of him at rest.
I watch Ireland blur past the window.

Fields and towns and villages and more fields, all of it streaked with that blue light, all of it moving past like a film that's running slightly too fast. The train clacks over a joint in the track — a sound like a heartbeat, regular and reliable. A couple across the aisle is sharing a sandwich. A kid two rows up is playing a game on a tablet, the volume just loud enough to be a tiny, tinny soundtrack.
And I think about the Garden. One more time.

I think about all the paths. All the branches. All the Averys who walked away from that Garden in different directions — back to their respective worlds, their respective timelines, carrying their respective griefs and hopes and versions of Julian Gallagher. Some of them chose to stay. Some of them chose to leave. Some of them made decisions I'll never understand and some of them made decisions I understand too well.
And I send them — silently, across the infinite branches of possibility, across the quantum foam and the probability webs and whatever else the universe uses to keep its options open — I send them:
*Thank you.*
*For showing me what I could be.*
*So I could choose who I wanted to be.*

Not because they were sacrifices. Not because my happiness required their suffering. But because every version of me — every Avery who stayed, every Avery who ran, every Avery who loved a different Julian or no Julian at all — every one of them showed me a face of myself I wouldn't have seen otherwise. And seeing those faces, I could choose which one to become.
The train rocks. The tracks sing their iron song. Julian's weight is warm against my shoulder and his breathing is the steady tide of a man who is home, even when he's asleep on public transport.
I close my eyes.

And somewhere, in a garden that may or may not exist, in a space between worlds that may or may not be real, a hundred thousand other Averys walk a hundred thousand other paths. Through cities I'll never visit and lives I'll never live and futures that branch and branch and branch again, infinite and uncountable and each one real in the only way that matters: because someone is living it.
Most of them will never meet. Some of them will be happy. Some won't.

But this one — this one sitting on a train in Ireland with her husband's head on her shoulder and the blue sky streaming past the window and the taste of borrowed rain still on the air — this one is walking home.
